content.body: Jake Archibald: did an editorial pass. Will let someone else verify the logic.
content.formatted_body: Jake Archibald: did an editorial pass. Will let someone else verify the logic.

content.body: annevk: are you happy with it landing without someone from WebKit verifying the logic?
content.formatted_body: @annevk:matrix.org: are you happy with it landing without someone from WebKit verifying the logic?

content.body: Yeah, that seems okay. If we hit issues when implementing we can always raise those at that point, but that is hopefully unlikely given the amount of vetting it will have had by then. (Assuming people still actually read the specification.)

foolip: seems like a long shot, but do you perhaps remember how the `muted` attribute of media elements is supposed to work? The description is essentially prose, which isn't great. It kinda suggests that script mutations should not work, but we don't really distinguish those from the parser setting attributes in the specification.
content.formatted_body: foolip: seems like a long shot, but do you perhaps remember how the muted attribute of media elements is supposed to work? The description is essentially prose, which isn't great. It kinda suggests that script mutations should not work, but we don't really distinguish those from the parser setting attributes in the specification.

content.body: The content attribute is reflected as `defaultMuted` and only affects the real `muted` IDL attribute at one point.
content.formatted_body: The content attribute is reflected as defaultMuted and only affects the real muted IDL attribute at one point.

content.body: (independent folks like Luke Warlow are looking at it)
content.formatted_body: (independent folks like @lwarlow:igalia.com are looking at it)

content.body: foolip: I think specifically the problem is this sentence: "When a media element is created, if the element has a muted content attribute specified, then the muted IDL attribute should be set to true"
content.formatted_body: foolip: I think specifically the problem is this sentence: "When a media element is created, if the element has a muted content attribute specified, then the muted IDL attribute should be set to true"

content.body: foolip: for instance, when cloning we'd first create the element and then copy the attributes, but the tests assume that .muted returns true for that case.
content.formatted_body: foolip: for instance, when cloning we'd first create the element and then copy the attributes, but the tests assume that .muted returns true for that case.

content.body: foolip: and the parser also does not create an element with all of the attributes at once.
content.formatted_body: foolip: and the parser also does not create an element with all of the attributes at once.

content.body: I guess I'll file a specification issue.
OK, so the issue is exactly when the content attribute is supposed to be read.
Ah, https://github.com/whatwg/html/issues/5013
Looks like Blink only respects the content attribute if set by the parser
annevk: is there establish spec language for special-casing the parser but not cloning?
content.formatted_body: annevk: is there establish spec language for special-casing the parser but not cloning?

content.body: foolip: https://wpt.fyi/results/html/semantics/embedded-content/media-elements/user-interface/muted.html suggests otherwise.
content.formatted_body: foolip: https://wpt.fyi/results/html/semantics/embedded-content/media-elements/user-interface/muted.html suggests otherwise.

content.body: And no, none of those subtleties are supposed to be available. But engines had them for optimizations maybe and it got never cleaned up and then features started to depend on it.
OK, a proper code search also finds that cloning is handled
Those are the two places the attribute is read in addition to the `defaultMuted` generated bindings.
content.formatted_body: Those are the two places the attribute is read in addition to the defaultMuted generated bindings.

content.body: Thanks for checking! I've agenda+'d it with some added context.
I linked the code in a comment. Does WebKit not do the cloning bits?

content.body: Luke Warlow: re "appearance:none still exposes some OS stuff I think", is there anything that's not "top layer"?

content.body: I guess `file`
content.formatted_body: I guess file

content.body: The range input's thumb is an odd one that I *think* might still be OS dependent
content.formatted_body: The range input's thumb is an odd one that I think might still be OS dependent

content.body:
But yeah also stuff like Safari file input which has image preview.

Perhaps we should define an appearance:none for file input anyway though? Seeing as we're defining appearance to apply to it for base mode
Luke Warlow: we probably can't change what appearance: none does for `file` for web compat
content.formatted_body: Luke Warlow: we probably can't change what appearance: none does for file for web compat

content.body: At least I expect making the button "appearance: none" fugly will result in user complaints for real sites
We could define it to render the button in appearance:auto and the label and not allow for stuff like the file image preview. Which is probably far less breaking?
But probably not worth it
Maybe
